questions.question: Explain the purpose of the 'typename' keyword in C++ templates with an example.
keyPoints: Introduced in C++98 to resolve ambiguity
Used when accessing nested types that depend on template parameters
Example: typename T::value_type to access nested type
Without typename, compiler treats it as static member

questions.question: What is a dependent name in C++ templates? When must 'typename' be used with dependent names?
keyPoints: Dependent name is a name that depends on a template parameter
T::X is dependent when T is a template parameter
typename must be used when accessing nested types
Not needed for accessing static members or functions

questions.question: What was the purpose of the 'export' keyword? Why was it removed from C++17?
keyPoints: export was intended for separate compilation of templates
Allows template definition in different file from declaration
Removed because very few compilers implemented it
Implementation complexity was too high
Modern alternative is header-only inclusion model

questions.question: Differentiate between 'class' and 'typename' in template parameter declarations.
keyPoints: Both are functionally identical in template parameter lists
class was used before C++98, typename introduced in C++98
typename is preferred for clarity
typename makes it explicit that primitive types are allowed
class can be misleading when using int or other non-class types

questions.question: What happens if the 'typename' keyword is omitted when accessing a dependent nested type?
keyPoints: Compiler error is generated
Compiler may interpret the name as a static member
Error message typically: 'expected typename'
The name is not recognized as a type
Requires explicit 'typename' to resolve ambiguity

questions.question: Describe the modern alternatives to the 'export' keyword for template code organization.
keyPoints: Header-only template implementations
Include model where definitions are in headers
Explicit instantiation with 'extern template' (C++11)
Include guards and inline functions
Module feature in C++20 provides alternative organization

questions.question: Write a C++ template function that demonstrates proper use of 'typename' to access nested types.
keyPoints: Template function with dependent return type
Use of typename T::value_type
Demonstrates accessing iterator_traits or similar
Shows correct syntax with typename keyword
Proper template parameter declaration

questions.question: Discuss in detail the 'typename' keyword: its introduction, purpose, usage rules, and importance in generic programming. Include examples demonstrating both correct and incorrect usage.
keyPoints: Introduced in C++98 to replace 'class' for template parameters
Primary purpose: disambiguate dependent names as types
Usage in template parameter lists (typename vs class)
Usage when accessing nested dependent types
Example: template<typename T> void func() { typename T::value_type v; }
Consequences of omitting typename
Best practices and modern recommendations

questions.question: Explain the 'export' keyword in C++: its original purpose, implementation status, reasons for failure, and current alternatives for separate template compilation.
keyPoints: export was part of C++03 standard
Purpose: enable separate compilation of template definitions
Only Comeau compiler implemented it fully
Deprecated in C++11, removed in C++17
Reasons: implementation complexity, compiler infrastructure required
Modern alternatives: header-only inclusion model, extern template (C++11)
Module support in C++20 as another organization method

questions.question: Write a comprehensive C++ program demonstrating template metaprogramming concepts including: (a) use of typename to access nested types, (b) template template parameters, (c) proper handling of dependent names.
keyPoints: Complete working C++ code
Demonstrates typename for nested type access (e.g., T::value_type)
Shows template template parameter usage
Illustrates dependent name resolution
Proper template declarations and definitions
Comments explaining each concept
Compilable and correct code

questions.question: Analyze the evolution of template keywords in C++ standards from C++98 to C++20, focusing on typename, export, extern template, and module features.
keyPoints: C++98: typename introduced, export defined but not implemented
C++03: export remained optional
C++11: export deprecated, extern template added
C++14-17: export removed from standard
C++20: modules provide alternative code organization
Comparison of approaches and recommendations
Future directions in template code organization
